excerpt_en: 2/22 So, since I was in Ireland and it was Saturday, I held a walking event for the first time in a while. For those who don't know, let me briefly explain -- it's an event group I started in Dublin. I hold events on an irregular
body_en:
2/22

So, since I was in Ireland and it was Saturday, I held a walking event for the first time in a while.

For those who don't know, let me briefly explain --

It's an event group I started in Dublin.

I hold events on an irregular basis.

The main events are:

Walking events,

Card game events,

Drinking events

This time it was a walking event.

The weather was perfect,

and about 15 people showed up.

We walked for about 60 minutes

while socializing,

and then chatted freely at a cafe.

It was a huge success again!

At this event,

Sho-san, a FOMUS community member

who came all the way to Ireland, also joined!

He half-jokingly said, "I came from Japan for this event,"

which got surprised reactions and laughs from the locals lol

Having a FOMUS community member other than myself

come all the way from Japan

to participate in an event I hold in Ireland was really great.

After about 3 hours of socializing,

we had a quick lunch

and then headed to the

Jameson Distillery for an Irish whiskey tour.

When it comes to sightseeing in Ireland,

it's basically all about alcohol.

This was my second visit too.

At the gift shop,

on my suggestion, he went ahead and splurged on a 4.5L bottle of Jameson.

He got himself a massive souvenir!

And then, we went straight for some Irish coffee.

The Irish coffee I had before at the Teeling Distillery didn't taste that great to me,

but the Irish coffee here

was incredibly delicious!

After that, we dropped our stuff off at the hotel.

And then, cheers once again at an Irish pub.

We kept drinking

until about 11 PM, I think.

For me too, having a friend from Japan

come all the way to Ireland

was a first,

so I was going all out with hospitality,

and it gave me a lot to think about --

it turned out to be a really interesting experience!

title_en: What I Learned from Giving a Tour of Ireland
excerpt_en: So, it's day 3 of FOMUS community member Sho-san visiting from Japan. It's the last day. So, we headed to Ireland's most famous beer -- the Guinness headquarters. The Guinness Storehouse is located in Dublin, the capital of Ireland
body_en:
So, it's day 3 of FOMUS community member

Sho-san visiting from Japan.

It's the last day.

So,

we headed to Ireland's most famous beer --

the Guinness headquarters.

The Guinness Storehouse is an experiential tourist facility for Guinness beer, located in Dublin, the capital of Ireland. It's a popular tourist attraction where you can learn about Guinness's history and brewing process while enjoying tastings and panoramic views.

This was my second time here,

but I remembered from my first visit

that they have a turtle in one of the advertisements,

so this time I brought along

FOMUS's character,

Masu-Game lol

And then, on the rooftop, there's the Gravity Bar where you can get a panoramic view of Dublin,

and we had a pint of Guinness.

The rooftop bar

is only accessible to people with admission tickets, but even so, it was incredibly crowded.

We didn't want to go during peak hours,

so we got there at 10 AM,

but sure enough, by around noon it was packed.

I made sure to snap a photo of Masu-Game x Guinness Beer too!

After that, it was lunchtime.

We went to a restaurant inside the Guinness Storehouse.

We ordered herb pancakes and steak.

Both were incredibly delicious,

and they paired perfectly with Guinness IPA.

It turned into a very elegant lunch!

After that, we went souvenir shopping for Sho-san.

I hadn't gotten a good look at

the Guinness souvenir section last time,

so we took our time checking it out.

Since I'm planning to start a capsule toy machine business,

looking at designs for small accessory items like these

is incredibly educational.

Always keeping my antenna up,

taking in all sorts of input,

and outputting it through articles like this --

it's a very healthy cycle.

After the Guinness tour,

I took him to a supermarket and an Irish chocolate shop

for souvenir shopping spots.

The weather wasn't great,

and we'd been going full throttle for 3 days straight,

so we relaxed at the hotel early.

*I was doing computer work though

Summary of the MaSU Special Tour:

So, that's how it went.

Over these 3 days, I showed Sho-san around

Ireland, taking him to

my recommended spots in Dublin

while he was here visiting during my stay.

I knew Sho-san was an experienced traveler,

so I wanted him to experience something different

from a typical solo trip.

Ireland's sightseeing

is more fun with two people than alone in many places,

so I accompanied him the whole time!

The whiskey tour,

the Guinness beer tour, of course,

and authentic Irish pubs --

definitely best with at least two people.

Furthermore, as something "only MaSU can do,"

I timed the walking event I organize

to coincide with his visit

so he could participate.

When you come to Ireland as a tourist,

"socializing" with locals

is quite difficult,

but in my case,

I've created my own event group

and I'm in a position to invite people,

so I have a differentiating factor.

I'm the only Japanese person who has

personally organized events multiple times in Dublin,

so being able to offer that unique experience was great too.

*Of course, people have different preferences,

so I only do this for people who are into it lol

So anyway,

we did a full-course 3-day program,

and I'm glad Sho-san enjoyed it.

It was a great learning experience for me too,

and it gave me a chance to objectively

see "my personal strengths" again.

These kinds of things are really important.

Also, if Sho-san hadn't told me he was coming during this period, I might not have been in Dublin at this timing,

and it was precisely because we locked in the schedule six months ago that I was able to attend the embassy event I was invited to the other day.

That was truly miraculously lucky.

So, in conclusion,

these were 3 days where I was able to articulate

my own strengths while giving a tour!

excerpt_en: 4 AM. Since Sho-san had an early morning flight, I walked him to a nearby bus stop. After sleeping a bit more, I checked out of the hotel and got to work on my own tasks. Since coming back to Ireland, I've been spending a lot of time meeting people
body_en:
4 AM.

Since Sho-san had an early morning flight,

I walked him to a nearby bus stop.

After sleeping a bit more,

I checked out of the hotel

and got to work on my own tasks.

Since coming back to Ireland,

I've been spending a lot of time meeting people,

and I haven't been able to do much computer work,

so today was the day to power through it.

Right now,

I'm working on flyer designs for the Sagi Island gallery project in Hiroshima, discussing artwork for the gallery exhibition, and so on.

Rebranding of the masu jewelry line "FOMUSPARURE."

And also,

finding people to meet in Dubai and Paris.

I posted a self-introduction

in a Japanese community in Dubai,

and several people reached out,

so I had some online meetings with them.

What was funny was

the warning from someone in the Dubai Japanese group that many consultants and company formation agents in that group are scammers lol

Sure, that does seem likely,

and they shared their personal experience of being "scammed" in an online meeting,

but honestly, I think

"it's the person who gets scammed who's at fault,"

so I'll take it as a reference,

but I can't definitively say at this stage whether the person they named as a scammer is completely bad.

I don't want to take just one side's opinion at face value --

my mindset is to look into things from multiple angles.

Especially since I have no plans to set up a company in Dubai right now, and FOMUS products are basically all made-to-order,

so I don't start production

until payment is received,

meaning risk management is covered.

Even if some too-good-to-be-true offer comes along,

I'm confident I won't be stupid enough

to fall for it and hand over money.

I built up this awareness

through various experiences in my early 20s.

So, that's how it went --

working while having meetings throughout the day.

Refreshing in the Evening:

Originally, I had two bottles of sake that Sho-san brought from Japan, and I was thinking of opening them and hosting a sake event today,

but I couldn't get enough

non-Japanese people to come, so I canceled the event.

Instead, I invited someone I wanted to talk to personally

for a quiet sake-drinking session.

I called up a friend from my graduating class and we had a great time toasting together.

I couldn't borrow proper cups or anything,

but in my case, I have "masu" cups with me,

so I've got sake and snacks all covered!

At the end, we ended up buying ramen on a whim.

It was a Japanese restaurant

that always has a line out the door,

so I'd been curious about it for a while,

but I never expected

to actually eat there at this timing.

This was something

I wouldn't have done on my own,

so when there are two people,

fun things like this happen --

I decided to hold these kinds of get-togethers regularly!

excerpt_en: So, from the evening, I attended an Irish business gathering held on the Dublin University campus. I wasn't just a regular attendee -- this time I was invited as a photographer. So I actually knew nothing about the event.
body_en:
So, from the evening,

I attended an Irish business gathering

held on the Dublin University campus.

I wasn't just a regular attendee --

this time I was invited as a photographer.

So I actually knew nothing about the event.

How did I even get invited?

Well, a friend of someone who had

participated in an event I organize

was looking for an event photographer,

and they reached out to me.

It was because I talked about how masu and cameras are my livelihood at the walking event that this kind of opportunity came about.

So, I had almost no info about the event!

My role was to take photos,

so I went around taking pictures,

but there was another photographer

shooting with a flash,

so I left the overall coverage to them

and I shot from an attendee's perspective with my DSLR.

There's no need for two photographers to shoot the same way,

so I shifted my approach.

Plus, I was told I could freely eat the food, so rather than fully committing to just photography,

I made time to socialize with the international attendees there.

I focused on photographing just the event presentations

and spent the rest of the time socializing like a regular participant.

As a result, I got to talk to all sorts of people.

This event was organized by

the business community JCI,

and the guest speaker happened to be Japanese.

So in a way,

I was able to attend at a perfect time.

This is what the event atmosphere was like.

These were all taken with my phone,

but I shot the event with my DSLR.

Honestly,

I didn't really feel a strong spark

from the Japanese business executive guest speaker,

which is my honest take,

but the fact that environments like this exist

was something I found incredibly interesting.

If I hadn't been hosting the walking events,

I wouldn't have had any connection to this event,

and it's precisely because I'm always challenging myself and staying active

that I was able to attract this opportunity,

so it was a very satisfying time for me.

A friend of the person who invited me --

she was also a guest speaker.

What I felt again was

that I want to speak English more smoothly

and be able to understand it better.

Everyone was doing business talk,

so the pace was fast...

I've grown a lot compared to before,

but I'm still far from where I need to be.

It was a time that made me realize I need to keep training so I can understand properly.

title_en: Becoming a Veteran in a Small World
excerpt_en: So, today is Wednesday. I was working non-stop during the day. During a break, I walked about 10 minutes to the supermarket and bought a sandwich. When I was attending language school in Dublin, I used to eat these for lunch.
body_en:
So, today is Wednesday.

I was working non-stop during the day.

During a break, I walked about 10 minutes

to the supermarket and bought a sandwich.

When I was attending language school in Dublin,

I used to eat these for lunch all the time.

You can eat for under 5 euros,

so it's relatively cheap.

Lately,

I hadn't been writing about everyday stuff like this,

so here it is lol

After a bit of a refresh,

back to work.

I was thinking about the brand strategy for the new "MASUKAME" launch, and one candidate is capsule toy machines.

I'm also considering placing one at

the art gallery on Sagi Island,

which is currently in the works.

If I set up a capsule toy machine,

I can sell merchandise

without needing electricity or staff,

and since the art gallery is planned to be unmanned,

I think capsule machines would be a really great fit. Plus, if it's a limited-edition capsule toy at an art gallery on an island you can only reach by ferry, wouldn't at least some people buy one?

As a mini-merch line for the MASUKAME brand I'm expanding, capsule machines seem like a solid business, and I plan to roll out MASUKAME capsule machines to other regions in Japan too.

And then, on top of that, I'll go after overseas markets.

That's the kind of stuff I was thinking about nonstop,

researching how to purchase capsule machines, costs,

capsule prices, merchandise items, etc.

If you don't set a time limit for this kind of thing,

time just flies by.

Heading to a Japanese Meetup:

Since it was Wednesday,

I went to the Japanese meetup.

This meetup is held every Wednesday,

and I've been attending since my first week in Dublin

whenever I don't have other plans.

I regularly hang out and drink with

the people who've been here a long time.

Wada-san, who runs an online community supporting Japanese people coming to Ireland, has been living here for about 8 years now,

and we've become good friends.

I also have an international exchange group in Ireland, and even though we're in different fields,

we're both community builders,

so I really enjoy talking with him.

Lots of other people came too,

and while I chat a lot with the regulars,

so many people have rotated in and out over the past few months.

So there were tons of people I didn't know at all.

Especially a lot of what seemed like college students,

and since I personally want to connect with working professionals,

I wasn't really inclined to approach them.

My own comfort zone

had formed even within this small Japanese group.

In other words, since there was a comfortable environment right in front of me,

I wasn't pushing myself to dive into new territory.

But that's not good enough,

and if I don't get more people to know about me, my business won't grow either,

so I went ahead and started talking to people.

There was someone who looked exactly like a person I met yesterday,

and when I approached them, they gave me a blank stare --

it was completely my mistake,

but that actually started a conversation,

so you really have to just dive in! lol

Thanks to that, about 3 hours flew by.

After that,

I went to the hotel where I'd been staying until today,

picked up my luggage, and headed straight to the airport.

I have an early morning flight tomorrow,

so I make sure to get there the night before.

*It saves on accommodation too

So anyway,

even though I've only been in Ireland for 8 months,

I've somehow become a long-timer in Dublin

and slipped into a comfort zone,

getting a bit slow to approach new people.

I'm glad I noticed it and was able to jump right into new circles,

but it just goes to show

that precisely because my relationships with people I know have deepened, I need to actively go talk to new people too -- it was a good reminder!

excerpt_en: Today is a travel day. The destination is Dubai. I waited at the airport from the night before for about 4 hours, then moved to the Dublin Airport lounge at 4 AM. As a Priority Pass premium member, I can use lounges for free. After having the familiar
body_en:
Today is a travel day. The destination is Dubai.

I waited at the airport from the night before for about 4 hours,

then moved to the Dublin Airport lounge at 4 AM.

As a Priority Pass premium member, I can use lounges for free.

I had the Dublin Airport lounge breakfast I've eaten many times before, and also tried the rather unusual experience of enjoying a Guinness first thing in the morning.

*The morning Guinness was way too much lol

Transit in Frankfurt -- An Unexpected Mistake

Dublin to Frankfurt (about a 2-hour flight)

I had about 3 hours of layover in Frankfurt, so I was planning to relax in a lounge.

However, there was an unexpected miscalculation.

The lounge I planned to use was only on the landside (before security), and there was no lounge on the airside (after passport control).

I had the assumption that "airport lounges = after security," and not checking properly was completely my mistake.

On this trip, I learned the important lesson: "Always check the lounge location in advance."

Istanbul -- A Packed Lounge

From Frankfurt, I headed to my next destination,

Istanbul. (About 3 hours)

There was a lounge on the airside here, so I went after arriving, but it was unbelievably crowded.

Perhaps because it was a pay-to-enter type of lounge, it was so packed that finding a seat was a challenge.

I had no choice but to move to a different lounge next door.

That one was also crowded, but I managed to grab a seat.

I rested while sampling Turkish food, though honestly I wasn't really sure what I was eating.

Even getting a beer was a struggle.

You had to order at the bar counter, but there were so many people that the staff couldn't keep up, and they were running low on glasses.

It was the most crowded lounge I've ever used, and it wasn't an environment where you could relax at all.

Istanbul to Dubai --

I departed Istanbul and finally headed to Dubai. (About 4 hours)

Three flights in one day -- a first in my life --

was way harder than I expected! lol

Why Did I Take 3 Connecting Flights?

If you use Emirates Airlines, you can fly direct from Dublin to Dubai in about 8 hours.

However, since a one-way ticket costs around 100,000 yen, this time I opted to take multiple short-haul flights to keep costs down.

On top of that, I planned about 3 hours of transit time at each airport so I could enjoy the lounges.

As a result, I arrived in Dubai for about 34,000 yen while making use of the lounges in Dublin and Istanbul.

Of course, the transfer procedures were a hassle, but this kind of flexible routing is something you can only do when traveling solo.

If I had been with someone, I would have chosen a direct flight without hesitation.

Saving on Flights to Upgrade the Hotel

With the money I saved on flights, I decided to upgrade my hotel in Dubai.

Dubai hotels, unlike those in Ireland or Spain, have that appeal of "I'd love to stay there at least once."

I figured, why not splurge a little?

Minimal Luggage with Tricks of the Trade

I traveled with just a backpack and kept extra charges at almost zero.

However, I stuffed everything from my tote bag onto my body to bring it on board.

Stacking transits like this and traveling with minimal luggage is by no means something everyone does.

That's exactly why I want to make this Dubai stay a meaningful one.

excerpt_en: So, it's the last day of February. Time really flies by. I arrived at Dubai International Airport at 3 AM, and after getting through immigration and everything, I got out at 4. It was still too early, so I waited at a cafe. Just this water cost 400 yen.
body_en:
So, it's the last day of February.

Time really flies by.

I arrived at Dubai International Airport at 3 AM,

and after getting through immigration and everything, I got out at 4.

It was still too early, so I waited at a cafe.

Just this water cost 400 yen. lol

Honestly, I thought it was super expensive,

but sitting in a comfy cafe chair for 3 hours

made it worth paying lol

Then, around 9 AM,

I headed to my accommodation.

I've prepared 3 different types of accommodation for this trip.

First is the apartment-style place I'm staying at starting today.

It's a dormitory about a 10-minute walk from the Dubai Mall, at around 4,000 yen per night -- an amazing location.

In other words, great location in exchange for sleeping in a dorm room.

Second is Couchsurfing.

I mainly used this during my American expedition,

and it's a service that matches guests who want to stay with hosts who want to host.

This time I've already arranged 2 nights,

and the main focus here will be interacting with locals.

And third, a 5-star hotel.

Nothing much to say about this,

but I'll use luxury hotels for just

the last 2 days, one night each.

This time, by getting cheap flights

and saving on accommodation through Couchsurfing,

I'm using the savings to upgrade to hotels at the end.

So, I took the metro from the airport.

Dubai's metro is like an unmanned monorail.

Super sleek.

The Apartment Accommodation:

This is where a room in a high-rise building or apartment

has been turned into accommodation,

like what I experienced in Kuala Lumpur.

Basically, a regular room

crammed with beds

turned into a lodging.

It has the bare minimum facilities.

For a solo traveler like me,

this kind of setup is totally fine.

Since I arrived at 9 AM,

check-in wasn't available yet,

so I dropped off my luggage first and went exploring.

To Dubai Mall:

What is Dubai Mall (The Dubai Mall)?

Dubai Mall is one of the world's largest shopping malls, located in Dubai, United Arab Emirates (UAE).

Opened in 2008, it covers a total area of about 1.2 million square meters -- roughly the size of 23 Tokyo Domes.

With around 1,200 shops inside, it offers not just shopping but also entertainment facilities and tourist attractions.

Main Highlights of Dubai Mall

1. Dubai Aquarium & Underwater Zoo

The world-class aquarium tank in the center of the mall features over 140 species of marine life.

The 48-meter-long tunnel aquarium is especially breathtaking.

2. Access to Burj Khalifa

It's directly connected to Burj Khalifa, the world's tallest building, and you can access the observation deck "At The Top" from inside the mall.

3. Dubai Ice Rink

There's a full-scale skating rink that's popular with tourists and locals alike.

4. VR Park

A theme park where you can experience attractions using the latest VR technology.

5. Dubai Fountain

The world's largest fountain show, located outside the mall.

It runs every evening, and watching the illuminated fountains dance to music is a must-see.

6. Restaurants & Food Courts

From fine dining to fast food, there are over 200 eateries.

You can enjoy Arabian cuisine unique to Dubai as well as cuisines from around the world.

7. World's Largest Candy Store "Candylicious"

For candy lovers, there's also one of the world's largest candy shops.

So, Dubai Mall is basically a place that

almost every tourist to Dubai visits.

This time, I got accommodation within a 10-minute walk from Dubai Mall, so access is perfect.

This was one of the places

I absolutely wanted to see,

and I finally made it here.

It's absolutely massive!!

I was hungry, so

while looking for somewhere to eat,

I spotted some biryani.

At 17 AED, that's about 700 yen!

I thought that was incredibly cheap and bought it right away.

I love these generous portions.

While I was eating this,

Dubai Mall's opening time of 10 AM came around,

and from there I explored the mall for about 3 hours.

The displays were incredibly stylish,

and they gave me lots of business inspiration too.

There were also several Japanese stores.

DAISO, Kinokuniya, and MUJI were all there.

And then, the famous aquarium.

People who pay for the aquarium get to see the giant tank up close,

while those who just come to Dubai Mall can view it from a bit further away.

Plus, you can take photos from the 2nd floor too.

*This photo was taken from the 2nd floor

On top of that, there's a giant screen on the tank.

It's powerful as advertising,

and by making the tank viewable for free,

they create awareness of the aquarium

and drive ticket sales as the back-end product.

Everything is so optimized for business --

it's incredibly fascinating.

Also, the DIOR special exhibition was amazing.

A golden, stylish space.

It really stands out.

There weren't that many customers inside,

but thinking of it as advertising, it's incredibly powerful.

So, Dubai Mall was way too big

to see in one visit.

Burj Khalifa is the world's tallest skyscraper, located in Dubai, United Arab Emirates (UAE).

Construction began in 2004 and it officially opened in 2010. At 828 meters tall with 163 floors, it towers over Tokyo Skytree (634m) and the Eiffel Tower (330m).

"Burj" means "tower" in Arabic, and "Khalifa" is named after the president of the UAE.

You can see it incredibly well from Dubai Mall.

If the timing works out,

I'm thinking of going up at some point.

I headed back to the hotel to check in.

The room isn't spacious,

and there's only a bunk bed and a locker lol

Since I hadn't slept in a proper bed for a full day, I was pretty exhausted,

so I decided to nap just a little.

Before I knew it...

it was 8 PM lol

I'd been out cold for about 6 hours!

I didn't want to waste the rest of the day,

and I was hungry, so I went back to Dubai Mall.

Dubai Mall is so huge

that there are food courts everywhere,

but it's so big I walked quite a lot.

What I found was fried rice.

It was marked down from 36 dirhams

to 27 dirhams, so I bought it.

That's about 1,100 yen.

It was properly delicious.

The luxury in Dubai is only for the last 2 days (5-star hotels), so until then, I want to explore more local food options.

So,

to cap off the night, I went to the fountain show

with a great view of Burj Khalifa.

You can also watch from the Apple Store balcony.

The fountain show was amazing too,

but personally I felt the Las Vegas fountain show

was even more impressive.

And with that, day one in Dubai was done!

title_en: Dubai Old Town & International Exchange Meetup
excerpt_en: 3/1 Day 2 of the Dubai exploration. I'm currently staying at a place within a 10-minute walk from Dubai Mall in the downtown area, but today I headed to Dubai's old town. It's called Old Town. This is Dubai's historic area,
body_en:
3/1

Day 2 of the Dubai exploration.

I'm currently staying at a place

within a 10-minute walk from Dubai Mall in the downtown area,

but today I headed to Dubai's old town.

It's called Old Town.

This is Dubai's historic area,

where in contrast to the modern high-rises,

you can feel the traditional Arab culture and commercial atmosphere.

I checked out the Al Fahidi Historical District

with its traditional architecture,

as well as the spice and gold markets.

I also found a Starbucks that's become a tourist attraction.

Building a system that draws people in

isn't easily done,

and this Starbucks has secured a prime location in the historic district, which is how it's become a tourist spot

that's always bustling with customers.

As a result, the juice shops around it

seemed to be losing business.

It seems the strong getting stronger

is the same in every world.

That's what the Bastakiya district was like.

Also, while walking around,

I got caught by a street hawker

and ended up being dressed in traditional Arab clothing.

It was a nice experience to try on the outfit,

but of course they tried to sell it to me --

and I was actually impressed

by how polished their sales pitch was lol

"At Dubai Mall it's 50,000, but

here it's only 20,000!

You're Japanese so I'll give you a special discount!"

Something like that. lol

They even use LINE.

I didn't want to exchange contacts but got roped into it anyway.

I had told them upfront that I had no plans to buy anything (my bag was already packed full),

but they did all of that anyway --

partly for business, I'm sure, but also to entertain me.

Thanks to that,

I got a funny photo out of it lol

Gold Souk & Spice Souk:

After that, I took a boat to the other side.

It was just crossing the creek,

so it only cost about 100 yen.

And here's the famous Gold Souk.

Every shop sells gold.

I'm not interested in gold,

so I just took a quick look and moved on.

There was also a souk selling all kinds of spices.

The aromas were really wonderful.

My Impressions:

Above all, it was packed with Chinese tourists,

kind of like Japan in the old days.

Japanese tourists are extremely rare,

yet for some reason, the hawkers

always greeted me with "Konnichiwa" -- which made me happy but also surprised.

#ThereAreHardlyAnyJapanesePeopleButTheySpottedMe

#EvenWearingSunglasses lol

I'm not really thinking about placing masu in Dubai's old town, but the display methods and souvenirs offered plenty to learn from.

Off to an International Meetup in Dubai:

In the evening, I headed to an international exchange meetup in Dubai.

Since I'm an event organizer in Ireland,

I've also been attending meetups in the countries I visit.

Today I just showed up on the spot lol

Of course, there were no Japanese people, so no Japanese --

it was all English communication.

Since moving abroad,

I've been diving into places where I know absolutely no one way too often,

and my English is still the weakest of anyone there,

but if I don't put myself out there,

I won't make any friends, so I actively participate.

*I don't take photos at these events, so there aren't any

As a result,

I made connections from Dubai of course, but also India, Kazakhstan,

Spain and more.

What surprised me was,

I'd heard you can't drink alcohol in Dubai, but apparently hotels and certain restaurants and bars

are licensed to serve alcohol.

I never expected to be drinking alcohol in Dubai,

so that was a surprise lol

In Dubai, English is used

as a matter of course,

so if you can speak English, it's actually quite manageable.

I'm still far from where I need to be,

so understanding conversations is incredibly tough every time, but it's something you just have to get used to,

so I'll keep throwing myself into places like this.

excerpt_en: 3/2 Day 3 in Dubai. I had no particular plans today, so I spent the day working at Dubai Mall. FOMUS stuff, jewelry stuff, and the upcoming MASUKAME (souvenir brand) stuff.
body_en:
3/2

Day 3 in Dubai.

I had no particular plans today,

so I spent the day working at Dubai Mall.

FOMUS stuff,

jewelry stuff,

and the upcoming

MASUKAME (souvenir brand) stuff.

So much to think about.

Just because I'm in Dubai

doesn't mean I can spend all my time out exploring.

The reason I'm able to travel and work overseas like this

is that I can't keep going if I don't generate income,

so I'm always thinking about

how to make the business work.

Writing articles every day like this is one of those ways.

I sell a 3-month bundle for 3,000 yen.

You can peek into my 3-month overseas expedition for just 3,000 yen -- quite a bargain.

I also run an online community and other things.

Basically, I launch all kinds of projects, and if something doesn't work, I shut it down or put it on hold.

I only keep the ones that show promise.

In my case, my hit rate is still quite low,

so I just have to keep trying different things.

There are plenty of things that haven't sprouted (or that I don't yet have the muscle to make sprout), but the experience and knowledge from those efforts feed into the next endeavor,

and I feel like all I can do is keep challenging myself.

Right now, being in Dubai,

I'm thinking that rather than living and working in Europe,

maybe working in a place like Dubai, where the country itself is constantly taking on challenges, would be better for my future growth.

I've only been to about 20 countries so far,

but the only places I've thought "I'd like to live here" are

Malaysia and Dubai.

*Europe is fine for tourism

So right now,

I'm researching things like:

How do I set up a business base in Dubai?

How much budget would it take?

Are there any subsidy-like programs?

And so on.

If I hadn't come to Dubai,

I wouldn't be thinking about any of this,

and being able to make time for this

is because I actually came here in person.

I found out that setting up a company in Dubai costs a lot of money, and since my business really depends on connections with local people,

ideally,

the best form of company would be one "recognized for its future potential by the Dubai government or Dubai-based companies."

I might also be able to build connections with Dubai investors.

I'm still just starting my research,

so I don't know how things will turn out,

but I'm going to keep exploring the possibilities!

excerpt_en: So, it's day 4 in Dubai. I was originally planning to explore the city today, but the person whose place I was supposed to stay at starting tomorrow messaged me saying, "You can come stay starting today!" So I checked out of the hotel one day early.
body_en:
So, it's day 4 in Dubai.

I was originally planning to explore the city today,

but the person whose place I was supposed to stay at starting tomorrow

messaged me saying, "You can come stay starting today!"

So I checked out of the hotel one day early.

Wait, did I actually know someone in Dubai?

Unfortunately, no.

Since my Dubai stay is a bit longer this time,

as I mentioned briefly in a previous article,

I used Couchsurfing

to find someone who'd host me for a few nights,

and we matched.

It was arranged about a month in advance.

We barely exchanged any messages,

but we're both the type who go by instinct on these things,

so it was all good!

So, he came to pick me up at Dubai Mall.

The person hosting me this time

was Karim from Algeria.

He's the same age as me

and lives in Dubai with his girlfriend.

On the drive back to his place,

we stopped by an Algerian shop

to pick up fruits and other groceries.

Then we headed to his place.

Honestly, I was just grateful to have a place to stay,

so I came without even checking what the place looked like,

but it was an incredibly comfortable spot.

Checking out of the previous hotel

one day early was definitely the right call.

And I just kept talking with Karim

the whole time.

He's apparently an English teacher,

so his English is super easy to understand.

For someone like me who's still learning,

his English is very gentle.

I really appreciated it.

After that, his girlfriend Federica came home from work,

and they told me about Dubai's tourist spots and recommended places.

There's information that doesn't come up on the internet --

stuff only people who live there know --

so just getting this info was amazing.

By the way, she's Italian.

I've been to Italy before

and to countries around it too,

so we had plenty to talk about.

Because we've all had various experiences,

the range of things we can talk about expands,

and it's really fun to chat.

Dinner was Arab Cuisine:

In the evening, we drove about 10 minutes

to the Dubai Festival City mall.

The three of us went out for Arab food.

Honestly,

this kind of exchange is a bit unusual.

You wouldn't normally meet these people on a regular trip,

and it's precisely because my goal is cultural exchange

that I take action toward it.

And that's why interesting encounters keep increasing.

So, the food:

From top to bottom...

Margoog (Margoog / Margog)

  • A stew-like dish popular on the Arabian Peninsula. It contains beef or lamb, potatoes, carrots, zucchini and other vegetables, simmered in a tomato-based sauce. Its characteristic feature is small flat pieces of dough inside.

Mixed Grill (Kebab)

  • A classic Arab grilled meat platter. In the photo, you can see chicken kebab (grilled chicken skewers), beef or lamb kebab (grilled red meat skewers), and kebab kofta (spiced ground meat). Grilled tomatoes, onions, and lime are served on the side.

Luqaimat

  • A popular sweet in the Arab world -- small donut-like fried dumplings. They're typically drizzled with honey or date syrup and sprinkled with sesame seeds. The UAE flag stuck in them shows they're served in Emirati style.

Aseeda

  • A traditional dessert eaten in the Middle East and North Africa. It's a smooth paste made from flour, water, and butter, eaten with date syrup or honey. It's especially popular in the Arabian Peninsula and Sudan.

That's what we had.

Everything was incredibly delicious.

After dinner,

we took a short walk around the area

and headed back home.

Even after getting back, Karim and I

got really into talking about music.

Making Friends Around the World -- Because I Made That Decision:

To begin with,

just a year ago,

I couldn't speak English at all.

The fact that I'm now having

normal conversations in English

would shock the me from a year ago lol

I was so overwhelmed by how bad my English was,

and to do business globally,

I went to the Philippines for 3 months to seriously study English,

then lived in Ireland for 8 months,

ran my own events,

and kept challenging myself with all sorts of things --

and now I've become someone who can

boldly dive into situations like this.

This time too, I'm staying at

a stranger's home,

but because the two hosts are such a kind

and friendly couple,

I'm able to enjoy myself like this.

I'm truly grateful.

So, for the next two days,

I want to soak in the feeling

of what it's like to actually live in Dubai.
